text: I'm Sam Svoboda, sophomore from Chicago and I'm majoring in urban studies. I'm also the president
of Stanford Curling this year. Curling is an ancient Irish field sport. It goes way back
into the Irish mythology. Some people estimate it was about 3,000 years old. It's been around
in Ireland forever, but really has not been able to spread beyond small Irish communities
abroad. We've been able to spread word of the sport throughout the entire Stanford community.
A lot of people are learning about it, asking us about it, and even if they're not playing,
they're getting into it and coming out to support us, which I think is really great.
Curling is an Irish sport. It involves the skills of lacrosse, baseball, rugby, soccer,
and basketball. It's a sport that's really important to me. It's a sport that I've been
into since I was a kid. I'm John Mulrow. I'm a senior and I'm from Wheaton, Illinois,
which is right outside of Chicago, and I'm majoring in earth systems.
My sophomore year, I taught a student-initiated course called the Irish Encounter. We took
a little field trip to San Francisco for the St. Patrick's Day parade and ran into a booth
on the Gaelic Athletics Association. They encouraged us to start a team at Stanford.
They said it had never been done, a hurling team on a college campus.
So we did.
It was pretty exciting this year. We actually played the first ever collegiate hurling match
in North America. That was back in January.
A huge contingent of the Irish community in the Bay Area rolled out to the game. We had
big crowds from both Stanford and Cal. It was a friendly game. Cal ended up winning
by one point in the last 30 seconds. We came back and beat them in the next game. The feeling
afterwards, there was local television there. It was very exciting.
It was a great feeling.
The money from the Stanford fund is huge for us in many regards.
A lot of promise for spreading not only cultural awareness and opportunities, but athletic
opportunities for the students. We need equipment. We need field space. We need jerseys and we
need to feel like, like I said, have that team experience. That's what the Stanford
Fund's money is going to bring us.
Every day is a new experience. We're all learning something new.
We're all learning something new.
And it's been a lot of fun. Truthfully, the group of 15, 16 people are my best friends
at Stanford. There's a great bond between the team.
We come from all different backgrounds and all over the country.
